title: End-Times Beliefs in Islam and Christianity
subtitle: Yawm al-Qiyamah and the Second Coming: Prophecies, Signs, and Eschatological Visions
summary: Both Islam and Christianity present rich eschatological traditions centered on divine judgment and cosmic transformation. Islamic theology emphasizes Yawm al-Qiyamah (Day of Resurrection) preceded by specific signs, while Christian doctrine focuses on Christ's Second Coming, the Rapture, and final judgment. This article explores the theological frameworks, shared symbols, and distinctive emphases within each tradition.

seo.metaTitle: End-Times Beliefs in Islam and Christianity: Yawm al-Qiyamah vs. Second Coming
seo.metaDescription: Comparative analysis of Islamic and Christian eschatological beliefs: Day of Resurrection, signs of the hour, Jesus's return, and final judgment.

sections.title: Overview: Two Eschatological Traditions
sections.body:
Islam and Christianity share deep roots in Abrahamic monotheism and, consequently, share concern with humanity's final destiny. Both traditions envision a divinely orchestrated endpoint to history, characterized by resurrection, judgment, and cosmic renewal. Yet the theological frameworks, timeline of events, and interpretation of key figures differ significantly.

Islamic eschatology centers on Yawm al-Qiyamah (يوم القيامة—the Day of Resurrection or Day of Standing), when all souls are resurrected before God to account for their deeds. This day is preceded by the Sa'ah (الساعة—the Hour), which Quranic and hadith sources describe as imminent, though its exact timing remains known only to God. Christian eschatology traditionally emphasizes the Parousia (Second Coming) of Jesus Christ, followed by judgment and the establishment of a new creation. Different Christian denominations offer varying interpretations of the sequence of events, particularly regarding the Rapture, Tribulation, and Millennium.

Both traditions regard these end-times events not as abstract philosophical concepts but as real historical futures grounded in divine revelation. Consequently, reflection on the end times shapes ethics, communal identity, and spiritual preparation in both faith communities.

sections.title: Islamic Eschatology: Yawm al-Qiyamah and the Hour
sections.body:
Islamic eschatology is anchored in the concept of Yawm al-Qiyamah, explicitly mentioned throughout the Qur'an and elaborated in hadith collections. The Day of Resurrection is presented as inevitable, divinely appointed, and—in its timing—known only to God. Qur'anic passages swear by this day and describe its cosmic magnitude.

The Qur'an emphasizes that this day will arrive suddenly and dramatically. All creation will be suspended, mountains crumbled, the earth transformed, and all souls raised to stand before God. The righteous will dwell in eternal bliss, while the unrepentant will face punishment. This doctrine is not peripheral to Islamic theology but central: the belief in the Last Day (al-Yaum al-Akhir) is one of the six cardinal articles of Islamic faith.

The approach to this day is marked by signs—some minor, occurring throughout history, and some major, appearing in its final stages. Minor signs include widespread sin, the cessation of knowledge, and social breakdown. Major signs include the Dajjal (an antichrist figure), Gog and Magog (Yajooj wa Majooj), the return of Jesus (Isa), and the sun rising from the west. Islamic scholars across centuries have compiled and interpreted these signs from hadith sources, producing rich exegetical traditions.

Crucially, Islamic tradition holds that Jesus (Isa) did not die but was raised to God. He will descend in the final times to lead believers, confront the Dajjal, and die a natural death before the end of the world. His return is both a sign of the Hour's proximity and a merciful act of divine guidance for humanity.

sections.title: Signs of the Hour in Islamic Tradition
sections.body:
Islamic hadith literature contains extensive accounts of signs preceding the Hour, but each sign should be tied to an exact report rather than attributed vaguely to 'the hadith.' Sahih Muslim 2901a lists ten major signs, including the Dajjal, the beast, Gog and Magog, the rising of the sun from the west, three major landslides, smoke, the descent of Jesus, and a fire driving people to assembly. Individual reports supply further detail and are interpreted with differing degrees of literalism.

Sahih Muslim 2937a gives an extended account of the Dajjal, Jesus' descent and defeat of him, and the release of Gog and Magog. Quranic passages independently mention Gog and Magog and the beast but do not contain the full later hadith chronology.

The Trumpet is Quranic, while identifying the angel who blows it as Israfil depends on later reports and interpretive tradition. The article should keep Quranic statements, authenticated hadith, and later synthesis visibly separate.

sections.title: Gog and Magog (Yajooj wa Majooj) in Islamic Scripture
sections.body:
The Qur'an references Gog and Magog (Yajooj wa Majooj) explicitly, weaving them into the narrative of Dhul-Qarnayn (often identified with Alexander the Great by classical scholars, though interpretations vary). The passage describing Dhul-Qarnayn's construction of a wall against these nations is one of the longest continuous narrative passages in the Qur'an.

The account presents Dhul-Qarnayn as a righteous ruler to whom God grants authority and resources. He builds a wall of iron and molten copper to protect communities from the destructive incursions of Gog and Magog. Dhul-Qarnayn acknowledges that this is a mercy from God, and when God's promise comes to pass (understood by exegetes as the end times), God will level this wall. The narrative emphasizes divine sovereignty: the wall will fall at God's appointed time, not by human effort.

Islamically, Gog and Magog are understood as literally existing nations, held back by this barrier until the Last Hour, when they will be released. Their emergence is among the major signs of the end times. The Qur'an states that they "descend from every height"—emphasizing their overwhelming number and irresistible force. Their release triggers one of the final upheavals before resurrection. Some classical Islamic commentators, such as Al-Tabari, offer extensive speculation about their identity and homeland, though Islamic tradition preserves humility about details not explicitly stated in Qur'an or reliable hadith.

sections.title: The Return of Jesus (Isa) in Islamic Eschatology
sections.body:
Mainstream Islamic eschatology expects the personal return of Jesus. Sahih al-Bukhari 2476 says that the son of Mary will descend as a just ruler, break the cross, kill swine, and abolish jizya. The report describes concrete actions; interpreting the breaking of the cross as the ending of false beliefs about Jesus is a theological explanation of the action, not a replacement for its wording.

Sahih Muslim 2937a places Jesus' descent within the Dajjal narrative and describes him defeating the deceiver. Other reports and later syntheses discuss the duration of his life, peace, abundance, and death. These details should be sourced individually because they do not all occur in one narration.

The Quranic basis is briefer and interpretive. Classical commentators often connect Quran 4:159 and 43:61 with Jesus' future return, while the detailed sequence comes from hadith. Islam understands the return as vindicating Jesus as Messiah and human messenger, not as God incarnate.

sections.title: Christian Eschatology: The Second Coming and Judgment
sections.body:
Christian eschatology centers on the Parousia—the Second Coming or Return of Jesus Christ—as the pivotal event that inaugurates the final age. Unlike Islamic tradition, which affirms Jesus's ascension and expected descent, Christian theology emphasizes that Jesus rose bodily from the dead, ascended to heaven, and will return visibly and gloriously to consummate history.

The New Testament presents the Second Coming as both imminent and mysterious. Jesus taught his disciples that no one knows the day or hour, emphasizing the necessity of spiritual watchfulness. Paul instructed the Thessalonians regarding those who have "fallen asleep" (died), assuring them that believers will be reunited with deceased loved ones when the Lord returns with power and a trumpet call.

Christian denominations, however, interpret the sequence of end-times events differently. Evangelical Protestantism often emphasizes the Rapture—understood as the sudden removal of believers from the earth before or during a period of tribulation—followed by Christ's final judgment of all humanity. Catholic and Orthodox traditions, while affirming the Second Coming and final judgment, do not emphasize a pre-tribulation rapture and maintain different frameworks for understanding the intermediate state and purgatory.

The common thread across Christian traditions is the affirmation that Christ's return will bring about the resurrection of the dead, the final judgment of all souls according to their deeds, and the establishment of God's kingdom in its fullness. Some traditions envision a literal thousand-year reign of Christ on earth (Revelation 20), while others interpret this symbolically. The certainty of Christ's return grounds Christian hope and ethics: believers live in expectation of his vindication and their ultimate vindication in his presence.

sections.title: The Rapture and Tribulation in Christian Theology
sections.body:
The doctrine of the Rapture—the sudden removal of believers before or during the Tribulation—is particularly prominent in evangelical and fundamentalist Christianity, though its emphasis and interpretation vary. The primary New Testament passage interpreted as referring to the Rapture appears in 1 Thessalonians, where Paul describes the Lord descending from heaven and believers being caught up in the clouds to meet him.

Christian interpreters disagree on the timing: some advocate pre-tribulation rapture (believers removed before a seven-year period of divine judgment), others pre-wrath (believers removed before God's final judgments), and still others post-tribulation (believers endure the tribulation period). These different frameworks have significant theological implications for understanding the nature of divine protection, Christian persecution, and God's purposes in the end times.

The Tribulation itself, derived largely from Revelation and interpreted through the lens of Matthew 24, is understood as a period of unprecedented suffering and cosmic disruption. Wars, earthquakes, famine, and cosmic signs (sun darkening, moon ceasing to shine, stars falling) precede or accompany this period. Many Evangelical interpreters identify a figure called the Antichrist or Beast who will arise during this time, deceiving the nations and demanding worship—a parallel to Islamic descriptions of the Dajjal, though with different theological significance.

After the tribulation, Christian theology typically affirms the visible, glorious return of Christ with his angels. He will gather his elect from the four corners of the earth, judge the nations, and establish his kingdom. This differs from Islamic expectation that Jesus will rule according to Islamic law rather than inaugurate a new covenant replacing the previous revelation.

sections.title: The Millennium and Final Judgment in Christianity
sections.body:
The concept of a thousand-year reign of Christ—drawn from Revelation 20—is subject to varying Christian interpretations. Premillennialism holds that Christ will return, bind Satan, and reign visibly on earth for a thousand years, after which Satan will be released for a final rebellion before being defeated and eternity established. Amillennialism interprets the thousand years symbolically as the current church age, understanding the binding of Satan as a spiritual victory already accomplished. Postmillennialism envisions Christ's return after a long period of Christian dominance and gospel expansion, with the millennium representing an age of peace achieved through Christian influence.

Regardless of millennial interpretation, Christian theology affirms a final judgment. Revelation describes a great white throne before which all the dead—small and great—stand to be judged according to their deeds. Those whose names are written in the book of life enter eternal life, while those not found in the book face the second death in the lake of fire. This judgment is comprehensive, encompassing all humanity across all ages.

The finality of this judgment underscores Christian ethical responsibility: deeds done in life determine eternal destiny. Unlike some theological systems that allow for post-death progression or multiple chances at redemption, classical Christian doctrine affirms that the judgment establishes irreversible, eternal outcomes. This sense of urgency and accountability shapes Christian moral teaching and spiritual practice.

The result of this final judgment is typically understood as the creation of a new heaven and new earth, where God dwells with humanity in unmediated communion. Suffering, death, and tears are eliminated, and creation itself is renewed. This vision of restoration and renewal, while differing in detail from Islamic depictions of paradise and hell, shares with Islam the affirmation that God's purposes ultimately triumph and that justice is finally and fully executed.

sections.title: Shared Symbols and Distinctive Emphases
sections.body:
Both Islamic and Christian eschatology employ symbolism of final judgment, cosmic renewal, and the vindication of the righteous. Both affirm resurrection of the dead, though they understand the nature and timing differently. Both present Jesus as a pivotal figure—though Islam denies his crucifixion and divinity, while Christianity affirms both. Both warn of deception and wickedness before the end, though the specific antichrist or dajjal figures operate in different theological frameworks.

Gog and Magog appear in both traditions, though their theological significance differs. In Islamic tradition, they are literally existing nations released at the end times, representing a punishment and test for believers. In Christian theology (particularly Revelation), they are forces of satanic deception and rebellion marshaled against God's people during the millennium or its conclusion. Their ultimate defeat in both traditions symbolizes God's final vindication.

Distinctively, Islamic eschatology emphasizes divine transcendence and the unchanging nature of God's justice and law. The end times in Islamic thought reaffirm Islamic truth and the prophecy of Muhammad; Jesus returns not to establish a new order but to witness to and support Islamic governance. Christian eschatology, by contrast, emphasizes Christ's personal return and the transformation of all things through his resurrection power. For Christians, the end times represent not merely the fulfillment of past revelation but the inauguration of a new covenant reality.

Another distinction lies in the intermediate state: Islamic theology offers less elaboration on what occurs between death and the Day of Resurrection (though belief in accountability in the grave is widespread), whereas Christian traditions discuss in detail the fate of souls after death—purgatory, paradise, and consciousness or sleep until resurrection. These differences reflect deeper theological commitments about the nature of justice, salvation, and divine action.

Within each tradition, moreover, significant diversity exists. Conservative and liberal Christians offer different readings of apocalyptic texts. Sunni and Shia Islam, while sharing core eschatological beliefs, differ in emphasis and detail regarding signs and figures. These internal variations remind us that both traditions are living faiths with ongoing interpretive engagement rather than monolithic belief systems.

sections.title: Theological Tensions and Interpretive Challenges
sections.body:
Comparative study of Islamic and Christian end-times beliefs reveals both resonances and genuine theological tensions. The most fundamental disagreement concerns Jesus himself: Christianity affirms his crucifixion and bodily resurrection as redemptive events and grounds for the promise of future resurrection for all believers. Islam denies the crucifixion, understanding Jesus as raised to God before his enemies could harm him, and his return as a return to human, prophetic mission rather than as a victorious resurrection-bearer for all humanity.

This difference cascades into different understandings of salvation and judgment. For Christians, judgment is mediated through Christ—he is both judge and advocate, having already borne humanity's condemnation in his death. For Muslims, God judges all souls directly on the Day of Resurrection; Jesus is a witness and supporter of justice but not the judge. The very structure of salvation history differs.

Another tension concerns prophethood and revelation. Christian eschatology culminates in the return of Christ and the establishment of God's direct presence with humanity (as depicted in Revelation's vision of God dwelling with humanity without temple or intermediary). Islamic eschatology affirms that prophecy ended with Muhammad and that the return of Jesus represents confirmation rather than innovation. The implications for understanding God's final will differ substantially.

Furthermore, Christian interpretive diversity on eschatological topics creates internal Christian debates that sometimes approximate Islamic-Christian tensions. Why did Christ not return immediately? How should apocalyptic texts be interpreted—literally, symbolically, or typologically? These questions have generated centuries of Christian theological work and remain contested.

Yet both traditions insist on several non-negotiable points: God is sovereign over history; justice will ultimately be executed; the righteous will be vindicated; and believers should live in moral seriousness and spiritual awareness. Recognizing both the genuine differences and the underlying shared commitments allows for respectful comparative theology without collapsing legitimate distinctions or imposing one framework onto another.

For students of comparative religion, the study of end-times beliefs illuminates each tradition's understanding of God's character, humanity's accountability, and the purpose of creation. It also reveals how eschatological convictions shape present ethics and community practice—not as escapist fantasy but as ground for hope and moral seriousness in engagement with the world.

quranVerses.arabic: وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَن ذِى ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ ۖ قُلْ سَأَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْهُ ذِكْرًا
quranVerses.translation: And they ask you, [O Muhammad], about Dhul-Qarnayn. Say, "I will recite to you about him a report."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَغْرُبُ فِى عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍۢ وَوَجَدَ عِندَهَا قَوْمًۭا ۗ قُلْنَا يَٰذَا ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّآ أَن تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّآ أَن تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْنًۭا
quranVerses.translation: Until, when he reached the setting of the sun, he found it [as if] setting in a spring of dark mud, and he found near it a people. Allah said, "O Dhul-Qarnayn, either you punish [them] or else adopt among them [a way of] goodness."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: قَالَ هَٰذَا رَحْمَةٌۭ مِّن رَّبِّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى جَعَلَهُۥ دَكَّآءَ ۖ وَكَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى حَقًّۭا
quranVerses.translation: [Dhul-Qarnayn] said, "This is a mercy from my Lord; but when the promise of my Lord comes, He will make it level, and ever is the promise of my Lord true."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَإِنَّهُۥ لَعِلْمٌۭ لِّلسَّاعَةِ فَلَا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِهَا وَٱتَّبِعُونِ ۚ هَٰذَا صِرَٰطٌۭ مُّسْتَقِيمٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: And indeed, Jesus will be [a sign for] knowledge of the Hour, so be not in doubt of it, and follow Me. This is a straight path.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ لَآ أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ
quranVerses.translation: I swear by the Day of Resurrection
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ ٱقْتَرَبَتِ ٱلسَّاعَةُ وَٱنشَقَّ ٱلْقَمَرُ
quranVerses.translation: The Hour has come near, and the moon has split [in two].
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَإِن مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَٰبِ إِلَّا لَيُؤْمِنَنَّ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ مَوْتِهِۦ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًۭا
quranVerses.translation: And there is none from the People of the Scripture but that he will surely believe in Jesus before his death. And on the Day of Resurrection he will be against them a witness.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُم مِّن كُلِّ حَدَبٍۢ يَنسِلُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Until when [the dam of] Gog and Magog has been opened and they, from every elevation, descend
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: رِجَالٌۭ لَّا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجَٰرَةٌۭ وَلَا بَيْعٌ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَإِقَامِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَإِيتَآءِ ٱلزَّكَوٰةِ ۙ يَخَافُونَ يَوْمًۭا تَتَقَلَّبُ فِيهِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ وَٱلْأَبْصَٰرُ
quranVerses.translation: [Are] men whom neither commerce nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah and performance of prayer and giving of zakah. They fear a Day in which the hearts and eyes will [fearfully] turn about -
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلْعَذَابِ وَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥ ۚ وَإِنَّ يَوْمًا عِندَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍۢ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ
quranVerses.translation: And they urge you to hasten the punishment. But Allah will never fail in His promise. And indeed, a day with your Lord is like a thousand years of those which you count.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَٰعِيسَىٰٓ إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَٰمَةِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ
quranVerses.translation: [Mention] when Allah said, "O Jesus, indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself and purify you from those who disbelieve and make those who follow you [in submission to Allah alone] superior to those who disbelieve until the Day of Resurrection. Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: As he sat on the Mount of Olives, the disciples came to him privately, saying, “Tell us, when will these things be? What is the sign of your coming, and of the end of the age?”
Jesus answered them, “Be careful that no one leads you astray.
For many will come in my name, saying, ‘I am the Christ,’ and will lead many astray.
You will hear of wars and rumors of wars. See that you aren’t troubled, for all this must happen, but the end is not yet.
For nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom; and there will be famines, plagues, and earthquakes in various places.
But all these things are the beginning of birth pains.
Then they will deliver you up to oppression, and will kill you. You will be hated by all of the nations for my name’s sake.
Then many will stumble, and will deliver up one another, and will hate one another.
Many false prophets will arise, and will lead many astray.
Because iniquity will be multiplied, the love of many will grow cold.
But he who endures to the end, the same will be saved.
This Good News of the Kingdom will be preached in the whole world for a testimony to all the nations, and then the end will come.
But immediately after the oppression of those days, the sun will be darkened, the moon will not give its light, the stars will fall from the sky, and the powers of the heavens will be shaken;
and then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in the sky. Then all the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of the sky with power and great glory.
He will send out his angels with a great sound of a trumpet, and they will gather together his chosen ones from the four winds, from one end of the sky to the other.
But no one knows of that day and hour, not even the angels of heaven, but my Father only.
“As the days of Noah were, so will be the coming of the Son of Man.
For as in those days which were before the flood they were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage, until the day that Noah entered into the ship,
and they didn’t know until the flood came, and took them all away, so will be the coming of the Son of Man.
Then two men will be in the field: one will be taken and one will be left.
Two women will be grinding at the mill: one will be taken and one will be left.
Watch therefore, for you don’t know in what hour your Lord comes.
But know this, that if the master of the house had known in what watch of the night the thief was coming, he would have watched, and would not have allowed his house to be broken into.
Therefore also be ready, for in an hour that you don’t expect, the Son of Man will come.
For the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with God’s trumpet. The dead in Christ will rise first,
then we who are alive, who are left, will be caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air. So we will be with the Lord forever.
But concerning the times and the seasons, brothers, you have no need that anything be written to you.
For you yourselves know well that the day of the Lord comes like a thief in the night.
For when they are saying, “Peace and safety,” then sudden destruction will come on them, like birth pains on a pregnant woman; and they will in no way escape.
I saw an angel coming down out of heaven, having the key of the abyss and a great chain in his hand.
He seized the dragon, the old serpent, which is the devil and Satan, who deceives the whole inhabited earth, and bound him for a thousand years,
and cast him into the abyss, and shut it, and sealed it over him, that he should deceive the nations no more, until the thousand years were finished. After this, he must be freed for a short time.

I saw thrones, and they sat on them, and judgment was given to them. I saw the souls of those who had been beheaded for the testimony of Jesus, and for the word of God, and such as didn’t worship the beast nor his image, and didn’t receive the mark on their forehead and on their hand. They lived and reigned with Christ for a thousand years.
The rest of the dead didn’t live until the thousand years were finished. This is the first resurrection.
Blessed and holy is he who has part in the first resurrection. Over these, the second death has no power, but they will be priests of God and of Christ, and will reign with him one thousand years.
And after the thousand years, Satan will be released from his prison,
and he will come out to deceive the nations which are in the four corners of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them together to the war; the number of whom is as the sand of the sea.
They went up over the width of the earth, and surrounded the camp of the saints, and the beloved city. Fire came down out of heaven from God and devoured them.
The devil who deceived them was thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur, where the beast and the false prophet are also. They will be tormented day and night forever and ever.
I saw a great white throne, and him who sat on it, from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away. There was found no place for them.

I saw the dead, the great and the small, standing before the throne, and they opened books. Another book was opened, which is the book of life. The dead were judged out of the things which were written in the books, according to their works.
The sea gave up the dead who were in it. Death and Hades gave up the dead who were in them. They were judged, each one according to his works.
Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire. This is the second death, the lake of fire.
If anyone was not found written in the book of life, he was cast into the lake of fire.

faq.question: Do Islam and Christianity agree that Jesus will return?
faq.answer: Both traditions affirm a return of Jesus, but understand it very differently. Christianity emphasizes that Jesus rose bodily from the dead and ascended to heaven; his Second Coming will be a glorious, visible return. Islam affirms that Jesus was not crucified or killed but was raised to God; his return will be as a prophet and as a human, not as a divine judge. These are fundamentally distinct theological claims about Jesus's nature and mission.

faq.question: What is the difference between the Dajjal and the Antichrist?
faq.answer: Both terms refer to deceptive figures in end-times prophecy, but in different contexts. The Dajjal (الدجال) appears in Islamic hadith literature and is described as a one-eyed figure who will emerge before the end times and mislead many before being slain by Jesus. The Antichrist or Beast appears primarily in Christian Revelation and is understood as a figure of rebellion against God who will demand worship. While both are deceptive agents of evil, they operate within distinct theological frameworks and eschatological timelines.

faq.question: Do both traditions teach bodily resurrection?
faq.answer: Yes, both Islam and Christianity affirm bodily resurrection. Islamic theology explicitly teaches that God will resurrect all souls in their bodies on the Day of Resurrection for judgment. Christian theology also affirms bodily resurrection, grounded in Jesus's own bodily resurrection. However, they differ on timing: Islamic tradition speaks of a single resurrection at the end of time, while some Christian traditions distinguish between the resurrection of believers (at or before the Rapture) and the resurrection of all humanity (at the final judgment).

faq.question: What do both traditions say about the timing of the end times?
faq.answer: Both Islam and Christianity emphasize that the exact timing is unknown to humanity. The Quran states repeatedly that God alone knows the Hour (Sa'ah). Jesus taught his followers that no one knows the day or hour—not the angels, but the Father alone. Both traditions, however, point to signs that will precede the end times. This insistence on unknown timing is meant to inspire spiritual vigilance and moral accountability rather than predictive certainty.

faq.question: How do Gog and Magog appear in both traditions?
faq.answer: Both traditions reference Gog and Magog, but with different emphases. The Qur'an describes them as literally existing nations held back by a wall built by Dhul-Qarnayn, released at the end times as a sign of the Hour. Revelation describes them as satanic forces marshaled against God's people in the end times or millennium. The symbolism suggests overwhelming, irresistible forces of destruction that ultimately succumb to God's final justice in both traditions.

faq.question: Do both religions teach final judgment by God?
faq.answer: Yes, both traditions affirm comprehensive, divine judgment of all humanity according to their deeds. Islamic theology presents the Day of Resurrection as the final accounting before God, with eternal consequences of paradise or hell. Christian theology similarly affirms final judgment, though the means differ: some Christian traditions emphasize Christ as judge, while Islamic theology reserves judgment to God alone (though Jesus will be a witness). Both traditions see judgment as just, comprehensive, and final.

furtherReading.title: The Islamic Understanding of the Hereafter
furtherReading.author: Dr. Umar Al-Ashqar
furtherReading.note: Comprehensive classical Islamic scholarly treatment of eschatological beliefs, signs of the hour, and the Day of Judgment based on Quranic and hadith sources.

furtherReading.title: The Last Things: Biblical Eschatology
furtherReading.author: Geerhardus Vos
furtherReading.note: Systematic treatment of Christian end-times doctrine from biblical sources, examining the Second Coming, resurrection, and final judgment across New Testament texts.

furtherReading.title: Christ in Islam and Christianity: The Representation of Jesus in the Qur'an and the Classical Muslim Commentaries
furtherReading.author: Neal Robinson
furtherReading.note: Academic study of Quranic and classical Muslim interpretations of Jesus, including his return, crucifixion, miracles, and birth.
furtherReading.publisher: State University of New York Press

furtherReading.title: Revelation and its Interpretations
furtherReading.author: Grant Osborne
furtherReading.note: Scholarly analysis of Revelation's apocalyptic imagery and major interpretive frameworks for understanding Christian end-times theology.
